1: A direct citation: "The fool has said in his heart, 'There is no God.' They are corrupt; they have done abominable deeds; there is no one who does good." Romans 3:10-12 quotes this verse (near-verbatim from LXX Ps 13/53, a near-duplicate of Ps 14) as part of the catena of OT texts proving universal human sinfulness: "There is no one righteous, not even one; there is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God. All have turned away, they have together become worthless; there is no one who does good, not even one." The universal indictment of Psalms 53:1 is the theological precondition for Christ's universal atonement: "since all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus" (Romans 3:23-24).
2: A revelation of God: "God looked down from heaven on all humanity to see if anyone understands, if anyone seeks after God." The divine omniscient survey of humanity — searching for understanding and God-seeking — and finding none is the universal negative that Paul's argument in Romans 1-3 establishes as the starting point of the gospel. The divine search of Psalms 53:2 finds one: Christ himself, who is the only one who fully understands and fully seeks the Father (John 4:34: "my food is to do the will of him who sent me and to finish his work"). Christ is the exception to the universal finding of v2, and through union with him, the sinful are found by the God who searches.
3: A direct citation: "Every single one has turned aside; together they have become rotten; there is no one who does good — not a single person." Romans 3:12 quotes this directly. The universal apostasy — every person turned, all corrupted, none doing good — is the scriptural warrant for the universal need of grace. Without the exception that is Christ, there is no escape from this verdict. But Romans 5:18-19: "just as one trespass resulted in condemnation for all people, so also one righteous act resulted in justification and life for all people. For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous." The "not a single one" of Psalms 53:3 is answered by the one whose obedience covers all.
4: A theme: "Don't those who work evil know anything — those who devour my people the way they eat bread and never call on God?" The cannibalistic metaphor for oppressors — devouring the people of God as casually as eating bread — is the OT's image of exploitative power. James 5:1-6 uses similar language about those who exploit their workers. The "never call on God" is the defining characteristic: the wicked have no covenantal relationship that would restrain their exploitation. Christ's community is constituted by calling on God (Romans 10:12-13: "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved") — the inverse of the characterization here.
5: A theme: "But there they are, overwhelmed with terror where there was no terror, for God scattered the bones of those who attacked you; you put them to shame, for God rejected them." The sudden terror of the wicked who had no reason to fear — the unexpected reversal of their confidence — is the eschatological pattern. 1 Thessalonians 5:3: "while people are saying, 'Peace and safety,' destruction will come on them suddenly, as labor pains on a pregnant woman, and they will not escape." The "scattering of bones" of those who attacked God's people is fulfilled eschatologically in the judgment that Christ pronounces: "Depart from me, you who are cursed" (Matthew 25:41).
6: A theme: "Oh, that Israel's rescue would come out of Zion! When God restores the fortunes of his people, Jacob will rejoice and Israel will be glad." The messianic longing — the psalm closing with a cry for rescue from Zion — is the hope that Luke 2:38 describes as the expectation of Anna and others like her: "giving thanks to God and speaking about the child to all who were looking forward to the redemption of Jerusalem." Paul cites this hope in Romans 11:26 ("all Israel will be saved, as it is written: 'The deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob'") — explicitly applying the Zion-rescue hope to Christ's future work. The "restoration of fortunes" of God's people is the eschatological restoration that Christ's return will complete.
